name: Lakshmidhara
dates_notes: Sixteenth-century South Indian Smārta commentator; dated by manuscript tradition and by his standing as the earliest extant systematic commentator on the Saundarya-Laharī.

lineage_polemical.note: Lakṣmīdhara's South-Indian Smārta-aligned reading of the Saundarya-Laharī defends the samaya (interior, conservative) sub-tradition of Śrīvidyā against the kaula (full pañca-makāra, including the antinomian elements) sub-tradition that Bhāskararāya later defends. The two readings frame the major intra-Śrīvidyā doctrinal split.

core_thesis: Lakṣmīdhara is the principal early commentator on the *Saundarya-Laharī* — the celebrated hundred-and-three-verse hymn to the goddess Tripurā-Sundarī traditionally ascribed to Śaṅkara — and his *Lakṣmīdharā-Vṛtti* is the standard South-Indian Smārta reading of the text. Lakṣmīdhara works within an Advaita-aligned theological frame: the goddess is the *Cit-Śakti* of Brahman, non-different in essence yet expressing herself as the manifest universe (*Lakṣmīdharā-Vṛtti* on [*Saundarya-Laharī* 1](cite://sankara/saundarya-lahari/1), *śivaḥ śaktyā yukto yadi bhavati śaktaḥ prabhavituṃ*, the foundational identity-statement). His distinctive position concerns the intra-Śrīvidyā *samaya* / *kaula* split: he defends the *samaya* (interior, ritually conservative) sub-tradition, on which the *pañca-makāra* are to be understood as inner contemplative substitutes rather than as the actual antinomian ritual *substances* of the *kaula* practice (*Lakṣmīdharā-Vṛtti* on *SL* 8–11, the *bhāvanā* gloss on the *kaula* references; on *SL* 41 on the *kuṇḍalinī* sequence read as interior *samaya* practice). The reading is consequently the canonical Smārta-acceptable form of *Śrīvidyā*, and the *Lakṣmīdharā-Vṛtti* is the standard text by which the *Saundarya-Laharī* is read in the southern Smārta tradition. The contrast with Bhāskararāya's later Vārāṇasī *kaula*-defending position is the principal intra-Śrīvidyā doctrinal split of the early-modern period.

engaged_works.title: Saundarya-Lahari-Vrtti
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored commentary; the standard South-Indian Smārta-aligned commentary on the Saundarya-Laharī.

ascription_evidence.description: Modern academic consensus; the Lakṣmīdharā-Vṛtti is the principal South-Indian commentary by which the Saundarya-Laharī is read in Smārta tradition.

engaged_works.summary: The standard South-Indian Smārta commentary on the *Saundarya-Laharī* — the hundred-and-three-verse hymn to the goddess Tripurā-Sundarī traditionally ascribed to Śaṅkara, organised into the *Ānanda-Laharī* (*SL* 1–41, on the abstract metaphysics of the goddess) and the *Saundarya-Laharī* proper (*SL* 42–103, on the goddess's manifest beauty). Lakṣmīdhara works through each verse identifying the technical Śrīvidyā references — the *śrī-cakra* iconography (*Vṛtti* on *SL* 11), the *pañcadaśākṣarī* mantra (*Vṛtti* on *SL* 32–33), the *bīja*-syllables, the *kuṇḍalinī*-cakras (*Vṛtti* on *SL* 9, the *mūlādhāra-to-sahasrāra* sequence) — within the conservative *samaya* sub-tradition. The interpretive signature is the consistent reading of *kaula* references as inner contemplative practices rather than as antinomian ritual substances: where the verses appear to gesture at *pañca-makāra* worship (esp. *SL* 8–11), Lakṣmīdhara reads the references through the *samaya* lens of meditative interiorisation (*Vṛtti* on *SL* 8–11, the controlling *bhāvanā* gloss). The commentary is the canonical Smārta-acceptable reading of the *Saundarya-Laharī* and is the standard text by which the hymn is studied in southern Smārta tradition. The contrast with Bhāskararāya's later Vārāṇasī Śrīvidyā position is the principal intra-Śrīvidyā doctrinal split of the early-modern period.

reception_notes: The Lakṣmīdharā-Vṛtti is the standard South-Indian Smārta commentary on the Saundarya-Laharī and is one of the principal documents through which the Śrīvidyā tradition enters the Smārta mainstream in conservative form. The contrast with Bhāskararāya's Saubhāgya-Bhāskara on the Lalitā-Sahasranāma frames the major intra-Śrīvidyā samaya / kaula split. Modern scholarship (Brown, Brooks) treats the two as the major early-modern interpretive options for the Śrīvidyā corpus.
